1:
“I am the true vine, and my Father is the husbandman.” The witnesses receive this as one of the great “I am” sayings, and dwell on the word “true” and the figure of the vine. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “true” (alēthinē) as “genuine,” the ideal of which the natural vine is a figure (Ellicott, Robertson). On where the metaphor arose, the commentators offer several guesses: that the Lord’s Supper just instituted suggested “the fruit of the vine” (Robertson; Matthew 26:29); that the golden vine over the temple gate caught the eye (Barnes); or that, crossing the valley toward Gethsemane (John 14:31), they passed a vineyard (Ellicott). Lightfoot draws the deeper contrast with Israel, so often called God’s vine (Psalm 80:8; Isaiah 5:7; Jeremiah 2:21): the old vine had degenerated, but Christ is the true vine into whom all who would worship God must now be grafted.

The Latin fathers read the figure of Christ as Head and his people as members. Augustine: the Lord “calls Himself the vine, and His disciples the branches,” declaring that “the man Christ Jesus is the head of the Church, and that we are His members”; for “as the vine and its branches are of one nature,” he “became man, that in Him human nature might be the vine, and we who also are men might become branches thereof.” Hilary sees him, on the way to his passion, unfolding “the mystery of His assumption of His flesh, whereby He supports us, as the vine does its branches.” Calvin, for the Reformed, gives the general sense: “we are, by nature, barren and dry, except in so far as we have been engrafted into Christ, and draw from him a power which is new, and which does not proceed from ourselves.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the foundation of the whole discourse on union: Christ is the source of all spiritual life and fruit, the true vine of which believers are the living branches, tended by the Father as the husbandman. Apart from him there is no life; in him is all fruitfulness.
2:
“Every branch in me that beareth not fruit he taketh away: and every branch that beareth fruit, he purgeth it, that it may bring forth more fruit.” The witnesses dwell on the husbandman’s two works and the searching question of the fruitless branch. The grammatical-historical commentators describe the vine-dresser’s twin labours — “cutting off all fruitless tendrils, and cleansing those that bear fruit” (Ellicott). On the unfruitful “branch in me,” the commentators largely agree it means not the unbelieving world but professing Christians who lack life: Gill distinguishes two sorts of branches, those of “historical faith” who are “in him by profession only” (like Simon Magus), and the truly fruitful; Clarke names “Judas, the unbelieving Jews, the apostatizing disciples, and all false and merely nominal Christians”; Robertson too refers it probably to Judas. Wesley reads the “purging” of the fruitful as cleansing “by obeying the truth” (1 Peter 1:22) and “by inward or outward sufferings” (Hebrews 12:10–11), so that “purity and fruitfulness help each other,” the reward of obedience being “to make us yet more holy.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, meets the question the verse raises: “Can any one who is engrafted into Christ be without fruit?” His answer guards perseverance: “many are supposed to be in Christ, according to the opinion of men, who actually have no root,” just as the prophets called Israel God’s vine by “outward profession” though they were faithless. The fruitless branches, then, are not true members cut off, but those who seemed grafted yet never drew life from the vine. The witnesses thus find in the verse both a warning and a comfort. The warning is that a barren profession, however near to Christ in appearance, will be taken away — fruitlessness is the mark of a branch that has no vital union. The comfort is that the fruitful are not spared the knife but pruned, the Father cutting back even the living branch that it may bear more; so that the believer’s trials are not rejection but the husbandman’s care, ordered to greater fruitfulness.

The witnesses note the comfort this holds for the suffering believer. The Father’s pruning-knife, which cuts back even the fruitful branch, is not the stroke of an enemy but the care of a husbandman who values the vine. Trials, losses, and chastenings are thus to be read not as signs of rejection but as the painful tending by which God makes his people more fruitful. The same severity that removes the dead branch refines the living one — to the end that it may bring forth more.
3:
“Now ye are clean through the word which I have spoken unto you.” The witnesses connect this to the pruning of v.2 and dwell on the cleansing power of Christ’s word. The grammatical-historical commentators note that “clean” (katharoi) keeps the vine figure — Clarke renders it “ye are pruned,” since “our Lord has not changed the metaphor.” Ellicott prefers “already are ye clean,” the pronoun emphatic, the cleansing accomplished “on account of the word… by reason of its moral power,” and not to be limited to John 13:10 but understood “of our Lord’s whole teaching” (cf. John 8:31–32). Barnes describes the long process by which Christ “had been under a process of purifying” the disciples — removing “their erroneous notions of the Messiah” and their love of “earthly honours.” Lightfoot and Gill find an allusion to the law of the uncircumcised fruit (Leviticus 19:23), the tree’s first three years’ fruit being unclean: “for these three years I have been preaching to you… now are ye clean.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the instrumentality of the word. Christ “points out the means of cleansing, namely, doctrine,” and “there can be no doubt that he speaks of outward preaching,” the word they had heard from his mouth — “not that the voice proceeding from the mouth of a man has so great efficacy, but, so far as Christ works in the heart by the Spirit,” the word “is the instrument” of cleansing. Wesley agrees: the word, “applied by the Spirit, is the grand instrument of purifying the soul.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a precious assurance and a doctrine of sanctification. The assurance is that the disciples, unlike the fruitless branch, are “already clean” — the pruning has begun, the word has done its cleansing work in them. The doctrine is that the word of Christ, made effectual by the Spirit, is the means by which souls are purified; so that the same word that exposes the fruitless cleanses the fruitful, preparing them to abide and to bear more fruit.

The witnesses draw the assurance latent in the ‘now.’ Christ does not leave the disciples in doubt whether they are fruitful branches or fruitless; he tells them plainly, ‘now ye are clean.’ The pruning has already begun its work in them through his word. So the verse offers the believer a present comfort: that the cleansing power of Christ’s word, received in faith, has done and is doing its purifying work, fitting the soul to abide and to bear fruit.
4:
“Abide in me, and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit of itself, except it abide in the vine; no more can ye, except ye abide in me.” The witnesses take this as the heart of the parable — the call to mutual abiding and the impossibility of fruit apart from Christ. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “abide” as continuing in living union with Christ by faith and love (Gill), and note the reciprocity — “abide in me, and I in you.” The illustration is exact: as the branch draws all its sap and life from the vine and can bear nothing “of itself,” so the disciple bears no spiritual fruit except by abiding in Christ. The Eastern Chrysostom marks that, having called them clean by his word, Christ now “taught them that they must do their part” — the cleansing is his gift, the abiding their continuing duty.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, dwells on the asymmetry of the two abidings: “they are not in Him in the same kind of way that He is in them,” yet “both ways tend to their advantage, and not to His.” For “the relation of the branches to the vine is such that they contribute nothing to the vine, but receive their support from it”: the branch gives the vine nothing, the vine gives the branch everything. So “the abiding in Christ, and the having Christ abiding in them, are both for the profit of the disciples, not of Christ.” Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the dependence the figure teaches. The witnesses thus find in the verse the governing law of the Christian life. The believer is not a self-sufficient agent but a branch, whose every fruit depends on remaining in the vine; severed from Christ he can do nothing, but abiding in him he bears fruit naturally, as the living branch bears grapes. And the union is mutual: Christ abides in the disciple as truly as the disciple in Christ — yet all for the disciple’s good, the vine ever giving, the branch ever receiving. To abide is therefore the one thing needful, the condition of all fruitfulness.
5:
“I am the vine, ye are the branches: He that abideth in me, and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit: for without me ye can do nothing.” The witnesses take this as the summary of the parable and its sharpest lesson of dependence. The grammatical-historical commentators note the restatement — “I am the vine, ye are the branches” — and the promise to the one who abides: he “bringeth forth much fruit,” the abiding union issuing not in a little but in abundant fruitfulness (Gill, Barnes). But the weight of the verse falls on the closing words: “without me ye can do nothing.” Robertson and the others render it “apart from me” (chōris emou) — severed from the vine, the branch is utterly fruitless and dead. The disciple can do “nothing” in the way of spiritual fruit by his own power; all comes from Christ. Ellicott and the commentators stress the absoluteness: not little, but nothing, apart from him.

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses this total dependence as the death-blow to all self-reliance and to every notion of fruit grown from human strength. Whatever the world may achieve of natural good, in the things of God the branch cut from the vine “can do nothing.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both the highest encouragement and the deepest humbling. The encouragement is the promise of “much fruit” to the abiding soul — union with Christ is no barren thing but the spring of abundant life and service. The humbling is the “nothing” — the believer is reminded that he has no resource in himself, that every good work, every grace, every fruit is Christ’s life flowing through him, and that the moment he presumes to act apart from Christ he is as powerless as a severed branch. So the verse drives the disciple back, again and again, to abiding: not to strive in his own strength, but to draw all from the vine, in whom alone is fruit, and without whom is nothing.

The witnesses observe how the verse balances promise and warning. To the abiding soul it pledges ‘much fruit’; to all it declares the sobering ‘nothing’ apart from Christ. Neither truth may be let go: the believer is neither to despair, as though fruit were impossible, nor to presume, as though it were his own. The whole secret of the Christian life lies between the two — to abide, and so to bear much fruit; for severed from the vine he is, and can do, nothing.
6:
“If a man abide not in me, he is cast forth as a branch, and is withered; and men gather them, and cast them into the fire, and they are burned.” The witnesses read this as the solemn warning that hangs over the parable. The grammatical-historical commentators trace the fate of the branch that does not abide: it is “cast forth” (eblēthē exō, Robertson’s gnomic aorist, the certain end), withers for lack of the vine’s life, and is gathered and burned — the dead branch, useless for anything but the fire. Clarke and Gill apply it to apostates and false professors, those attached to Christ only by outward profession who, never drawing life from him, are at last separated and destroyed. Ellicott and the others note the terrible series — cast out, withered, gathered, burned — depicting the end of a fruitless profession.

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads it of those who “seem to be engrafted” yet have no root, and finds in it a warning against false security: a profession that does not abide and bear fruit ends in the fire. The witnesses note the debate the verse touches — whether it teaches the loss of a real salvation or the exposure of one never truly possessed — but agree on its practical force. The withered branch is the image of every soul that, once near to Christ in outward connection, fails to abide in him by living faith. The witnesses thus find in the verse the dark counterpart to the promise of much fruit. To abide is life and fruitfulness; not to abide is to wither and burn. The warning is meant not to drive the trembling believer to despair but to drive every professor to examine whether he truly abides — for the only safety is in the vine, and the branch that does not remain in Christ has no life of its own, but is fit only to be cast away and consumed.

The witnesses stress that the warning is addressed to professors, to press self-examination, not to rob the trusting of their peace. The branch that withers is the one that never truly drew life from the vine; and the remedy held out is not anxious striving but abiding. So the verse turns the dread of being cast away into an incentive to remain in Christ, in whom alone is life — for the only branches that wither are those that do not abide.
7:
“If ye abide in me, and my words abide in you, ye shall ask what ye will, and it shall be done unto you.” The witnesses dwell on the conditions of prevailing prayer and the new turn the abiding takes. The grammatical-historical commentators note that here Christ defines the mutual abiding of v.4 from a fresh angle: where we might expect “and I in you,” he says “and my words abide in you,” for, as Ellicott observes, “the abiding of His words in them is the means by which, and the proof that, they do abide in Him.” Barnes describes the word abiding “not only remembered, but suffered to remain… as a living principle, to regulate your affections and life.” On the great promise — “ask what ye will, and it shall be done” — Robertson calls it an “astounding command and promise… not without conditions and limitations,” for it presupposes “such intimate union and harmony with Christ that nothing will be asked out of accord with the mind of Christ.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, applies the promise to the believer’s felt poverty: since “believers often feel that they are starved, and are very far from that rich fatness which is necessary for yielding abundant fruit,” Christ assures them that “there is a remedy provided for their poverty, as soon as they ask it from God.” He adds the pastoral note that “the Lord often suffers us to hunger, in order to train us to earnestness in prayer.” Wesley sums it: “prayers themselves are a fruit of faith, and they produce more fruit.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the link between abiding and asking. The disciple who remains in Christ, with Christ’s words remaining in him, is so brought into harmony with the divine will that his prayers are framed by it and answered through it. Such a one may “ask what he will,” for his will, shaped by the indwelling word, asks only what accords with Christ — and so his prayer becomes the channel of the very fruitfulness the vine is meant to bear.

The witnesses note that the promise is no charter for self-will. Because it is hung upon abiding and the indwelling word, it shapes the very desires of the one who prays, so that he asks not his own fancies but what accords with Christ. The more deeply a soul abides, the more its will is conformed to the Lord’s, and the more freely it may ask, certain of an answer. Thus the boldest promise of prayer is also the safest, fenced by the condition of union.
8:
“Herein is my Father glorified, that ye bear much fruit; so shall ye be my disciples.” The witnesses find in this both the end of fruitfulness — God’s glory — and its evidence of discipleship. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the believer’s much fruit honours the Father: Barnes lists how — it “shows the excellence of his law which requires it,” “the power of his gospel… which can overcome the evil propensities of the heart,” and the restoring of the Christian to God’s image. Clarke puts it homely: “it is the honour of the husbandman to have good, strong, vigorous vines, plentifully laden with fruit; so it is the honour of God to have strong, vigorous, holy children.” Robertson reads “herein” of “the vital union and the much fruit bearing,” pointing “backwards and forwards,” and the clause “so shall ye be (rather, become) my disciples” — fruit is the proof of true discipleship.

The Eastern Chrysostom draws out the security in the promise: if the disciples’ fruit-bearing “appertains to My Father’s glory,” then “He will not despise His own glory” but will keep them fruitful and invincible. Augustine, for the Latin fathers, finds in the verse a commendation of “the grace whereby we are saved,” glory and grace being joined. Calvin, for the Reformed, gathers it as a confirmation of the prayer-promise: since fruitfulness “contributes very greatly to his glory,” God will surely hear the prayers of those who “desire to be rendered fruitful”; and the same end “kindles in them the desire of doing good.” The witnesses thus find the verse binding fruitfulness, the Father’s glory, and discipleship into one. The much fruit the abiding branch bears is no mere private benefit but the glory of God, the proof that the law is good, the gospel mighty, and the believer truly Christ’s. To bear much fruit is at once to glorify the Father and to be shown a disciple indeed — so that the disciple’s fruitfulness and God’s glory are not rivals but one.

The witnesses draw the encouragement that fruitfulness and God’s glory are bound together, so the believer need not fear that his fruit-bearing is for his own sake alone. In bearing much fruit he glorifies the Father and is shown a true disciple; his usefulness and his Lord’s honour rise together. The verse thus dignifies the humblest fruit of grace as an offering to God, and assures the abiding soul that its labour, however small it seems, is the very glory of the Father.
9:
“As the Father hath loved me, so have I loved you: continue ye in my love.” The witnesses dwell on the chain of love and the command to abide in it. The grammatical-historical commentators marvel at the measure of Christ’s love — “as the Father hath loved me.” Barnes: “the love of the Father toward his only-begotten Son is the highest affection of which we can conceive… yet this love is adduced to illustrate the tender affection which the Lord Jesus has for all his friends.” Ellicott traces the love passing from the Father to the Son and “from the Son ever going forth to all who would receive it,” the very “foundation of their union with Him and with God.” Henry calls the verse a discourse on “a fourfold love,” beginning with the Father’s love to Christ. Robertson notes the constative imperative “abide” (meinate) “in the love that I have for you,” and that “our love for Christ is the result of Christ’s love for us, and is grounded at bottom in the Father’s love for the world” (John 3:16).

Calvin, for the Reformed, would not have the words “philosophized away” into an abstruse inquiry about the eternal love within the Godhead, for Christ’s “design was rather to lay, as it were, in our bosom a sure pledge of God’s love towards us.” Wesley reads the command tenderly and pointedly: “keep your place in my affection; see that ye do not forfeit that invaluable blessing.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the staggering height of the love that holds the disciple. The same love with which the Father has loved the Son — the highest, eternal, perfect love — is the love with which Christ loves his own; and the believer is bidden to “continue” in it, to remain within its warmth, as the branch in the vine. To abide in Christ is, at bottom, to abide in his love, and that love is no creaturely affection but the very love of the Father resting on the Son and through the Son on us.
10:
“If ye keep my commandments, ye shall abide in my love; even as I have kept my Father’s commandments, and abide in his love.” The witnesses take this as the path of perseverance in love — obedience — set forth in Christ’s own example. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that keeping the commandments is “the outward proof of love towards Him,” and so “the condition of abiding in that love” (Ellicott; cf. John 14:21). Robertson notes the future “ye will abide” as the conclusion of the condition, and that this is “the correlative of John 14:15” — love and keeping the commandments each involving the other. Gill is careful that obedience is “not the cause or reason” of the believer’s interest in Christ’s love, but the way in which he enjoys its “further discoveries.” Clarke draws the practical point: “it is impossible to retain a sense of God’s pardoning love without continuing in the obedience of faith.” And all note the high example: Christ himself “kept my Father’s commandments, and abide in his love.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, points to “the method of perseverance”: it is “to follow where he calls,” for “they who are in Christ walk not according to the flesh, but according to the Spirit” (Romans 8:1), faith and a good conscience being “continually united.” Christ does not reconcile believers to the Father “that they may indulge in wickedness without reserve.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the law of abiding love. The love of Christ is not held by a passive sentiment but by an active obedience; the disciple remains in that love by keeping the commandments, as the Son remains in the Father’s love by perfect obedience to him. So the verse joins love and obedience inseparably, and sets the Son’s own filial obedience as the pattern: as Christ abides in the Father’s love by doing his will, so the believer abides in Christ’s love by keeping his word — obedience the evidence and the channel of an abiding love.

The witnesses underline that the verse forbids a love that does not obey. To claim to abide in Christ’s love while disregarding his commandments is to deceive oneself; the love is kept by keeping the word. Yet the obedience is not a cold legalism but the natural expression of a love received, and it follows the pattern of the Son, who abides in the Father’s love by perfect obedience. So the believer is called not to earn that love but to remain in it, by walking in the way it marks out.
11:
“These things have I spoken unto you, that my joy might remain in you, and that your joy might be full.” The witnesses find in this the gracious purpose of the whole discourse — the fullness of the disciples’ joy. The grammatical-historical commentators note that “these things” gathers up the teaching on the vine, the abiding, and the love (Gill, Barnes): all has been spoken not to burden but to gladden. “My joy” Barnes takes of the happiness Christ would impart, and Robertson of a joy “fulfilled” — brought to its fullness in them. The aim is twofold: that Christ’s own joy might “remain” in them, an abiding gladness, and that their joy might be “full,” lacking nothing. Far from the gloom the disciples felt at his departure, Christ’s words are meant to leave them rejoicing.

The Eastern Chrysostom adds a warning that the joy must be carried to the end: as “a vessel of immense burden, if it reach not the harbour in time, but founder in the midst of the sea, gains nothing from the length of the voyage,” so souls “which fall back when near the end of their labours” lose their joy; the fullness of joy is for those who abide and persevere. Augustine, for the Latin fathers, links the joy to the love just commanded. Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find here the tender intention behind every hard saying of the night. The witnesses thus read the verse as the key to the discourse’s tone. Christ has spoken of his going, of the vine and the pruning, of obedience and the world’s hatred to come — yet his end in all is joy: that his own joy, the joy of union with the Father, might dwell in his disciples, and that their joy might be made complete. The fruit of abiding in Christ and in his love is not a grudging duty but a full and lasting gladness, the very joy of Christ himself remaining in the believer.

The witnesses dwell on the tenderness of Christ’s aim. On the very night of his betrayal, with the cross before him, his thought is for the joy of his disciples — that his own gladness might fill them and overflow. The verse thus colours the whole discourse: its commands and warnings are spoken not to weigh the heart down but to lift it up, that the fruit of abiding might be a joy full and lasting. There is no truer happiness than the joy of Christ remaining in the soul.
12:
“This is my commandment, That ye love one another, as I have loved you.” The witnesses recognise here the return of the new commandment of John 13:34, now made central. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Christ singles out one commandment above the rest — mutual love — as the sum of the disciples’ duty to one another, and fixes its standard: “as I have loved you.” The measure is not love as we love ourselves merely, but love as Christ has loved us — a love that serves, stoops, and (as the next verse will say) lays down its life. Ellicott and the others observe that this love is the fruit and the proof of abiding in Christ’s love (v.9): those who remain in his love will love one another with the love they have received.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, recalls that Christ “had already made this same announcement” as “a new commandment,” and that the love commanded is the very love with which he has loved us — the pattern and the power of all Christian love. Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find in the singling-out of this one commandment the heart of the law of Christ. The witnesses thus read the verse as the practical crown of the vine-discourse. Having taught the disciples to abide in him and in his love, Christ now tells them how that love must show itself — in love to one another, after the measure of his own love for them. Mutual love is not one duty among many but “my commandment,” the distinctive mark of his people and the visible fruit of their union with the vine. To abide in Christ is to be filled with his love; and the love so received is to flow out, in the same self-giving measure, toward the brethren.

The witnesses press the searching standard of the command. It is easy to profess love for the brethren; it is another thing to love them ‘as I have loved you’ — with a love that forgives, serves, and spends itself. By fixing his own love as the measure, Christ leaves no room for a thin or selective affection. The mutual love he commands is the very life of the church and the surest evidence that his love abides in his people; where it is wanting, the branch may well doubt its union with the vine.
13:
“Greater love hath no man than this, that a man lay down his life for his friends.” The witnesses take this as Christ’s definition of the highest love and the measure of his own. The grammatical-historical commentators agree that “no higher expression of love could be given” (Barnes): “life is the most valuable object we possess,” so to lay it down for another “shows the utmost extent of love.” Clarke notes that “no man can carry his love for his friend farther than this,” and that Christ adds, in effect, “this proof of my love for you I shall give in a few hours” — the doctrine “I am just going to exemplify myself.” Both Barnes and Clarke recall the rare heathen examples (Damon and Pythias) of such friendship, the more to magnify the love that surpasses them. Ellicott marks that “nothing greater is conceivable in the thought of love,” and that Christ has set “His own love for them as the measure of their love for each other” (cf. 1 John 3:16); Robertson links the phrase to the good shepherd who “lays down his life for the sheep” (John 10:11; cf. Romans 5:7–8).

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, finds here the Lord defining “that fullness of love which we ought to bear to one another,” set between the command to love (v.12) and the naming of the disciples as friends (v.14). Calvin, for the Reformed, notes that Christ “proclaims the greatness of his love to us” both to “confirm our confidence in our salvation” and “to inflame us, by his example, to love the brethren” — for we are to “taste by faith how inestimably delightful his goodness is,” and so be drawn “to cultivate brotherly love.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both the measure of Christian love and its motive. The measure is the laying down of life — the love Christ commands among his people is no less than the love he is about to show on the cross. The motive is his own example: having loved us to the death, he both shows us what love is and stirs us, by that love received, to love one another in the same self-giving way.
14:
“Ye are my friends, if ye do whatsoever I command you.” The witnesses dwell on the dignity of friendship with Christ and the obedience that marks it. The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphatic “ye are my friends” — “ye are those… for whom I am about to give the greatest proof of love” (Ellicott). Robertson reads the third-class condition “if ye keep on doing” — “not just spasmodic obedience” — and observes that “obedience to Christ’s commands is a prerequisite to discipleship and fellowship,” repeated in the Great Commission (Matthew 28:20). Gill marks that the title “friends” belongs “not only to his apostles, but to all that love him, believe in him, and obey him,” to whom he has shown himself friendly “by laying down his life.” Barnes notes that, where the common title had been “servants,” Christ now addresses them as friends.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, marvels at “what great condescension!” — that the very obedience by which men are proved “good servants” Christ makes the mark of friends. Calvin, for the Reformed, guards against pride: Christ “does not mean that we obtain so great an honour by our own merit, but only reminds them of the condition on which he receives us into favour,” as in v.10 — for grace teaches us to “live soberly, and righteously, and piously.” Wesley, for the Wesleyan, hears in the “if” a needed corrective: “on this condition, not otherwise — a thunderbolt for Antinomianism! Who then dares assert that God’s love does not at all depend on man’s works?” The witnesses thus find in the verse both a high honour and a sober condition. The honour is unspeakable — the disciples are not slaves but friends of the Lord of glory. The condition is obedience: this friendship is no licence for self-will, but a fellowship of love expressed in keeping his commands. To be Christ’s friend is to do what he commands, not as a hireling earning wages, but as one whose love is proved by glad obedience.

The witnesses note how the verse holds together intimacy and reverence. To be called Christ’s friend is the height of honour, yet it does not abolish his lordship: the friend is known by doing what the Lord commands. So the believer is neither a cowering slave nor a presumptuous equal, but a friend whose love is shown in obedience. The condition guards the friendship from becoming a cover for self-will, and roots it where it belongs — in glad submission to the One who first laid down his life for his friends.
15:
“Henceforth I call you not servants; for the servant knoweth not what his lord doeth: but I have called you friends; for all things that I have heard of my Father I have made known unto you.” The witnesses dwell on the new dignity of friendship and the intimacy of revelation that grounds it. The grammatical-historical commentators note the change of title: Robertson reads “no longer” (ouketi) and the perfect “I have called you friends,” “a permanent state of new dignity,” though Paul still gloried in being Christ’s bond-slave. The mark of the difference is knowledge: “the servant knoweth not what his lord doeth,” but Christ has “made known” to his friends “all things that I have heard of my Father.” Lightfoot recalls that Abraham was “the friend of God” (Genesis 18:17), to whom God revealed his purpose. Gill marks the move from the “servile spirit” of the legal dispensation to the freedom of friendship now that Christ is come.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, asks how to understand “all things that I have heard of my Father,” since no man can comprehend all the Son has heard from the Father — and answers that Christ made known what was needful and fitting for them. Origen reads the contrast of servant and friend as the difference between “the spirit of bondage to fear” and the freedom of those who “make progress and hasten towards wisdom.” Calvin, for the Reformed, takes the verse as another proof of Christ’s love: he “opened his mind fully to them, as familiar communication is maintained among friends,” so that this is “a pledge of my love toward you, that I have… explained to you the secrets of heavenly wisdom which I had heard from the Father.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the marvel of the gospel’s intimacy. Under the old covenant men served as slaves who knew not their master’s mind; now Christ admits his own to the confidence of friends, sharing with them the counsels of the Father. The frank disclosure of the Father’s will is the badge of this friendship — that the Lord no longer keeps his servants at a distance, but takes them into his confidence, making known to them the things of God.
16:
“Ye have not chosen me, but I have chosen you, and ordained you, that ye should go and bring forth fruit, and that your fruit should remain: that whatsoever ye shall ask of the Father in my name, he may give it you.” The witnesses dwell on the priority of Christ’s choice and the ends to which he chose. The grammatical-historical commentators note the reversal of custom: Lightfoot recalls the rabbinic maxim, “choose to thyself a master,” for among the Jews “the disciple should choose his own master,” whereas Christ chose his disciples (Gill). Barnes observes that “chosen” is the word from which “elect” is derived, and refers it “doubtless… to his choosing or electing them to be apostles” (John 6:70); Robertson notes the aorist “I chose you,” recalling the night of prayer before the choice (Luke 6:13). The ends are three: “that ye should go and bring forth fruit,” that “your fruit should remain” (an abiding, lasting harvest, Wesley: “to the end of the world; yea, to eternity”), and that their prayers in his name should be answered.

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the doctrine of grace the verse declares: Christ “declares still more clearly that it must not be ascribed to their own merit, but to his grace, that they have arrived at so great an honour,” for “whatever they have they did not obtain by their own skill or industry.” Against those who “imagine some kind of concurrence to take place between the grace of God and the will of man,” the words “ye have not chosen me, but I have chosen you” claim “exclusively for Christ alone” the whole of our calling. The witnesses thus find in the verse the foundation of all the disciples’ standing in free, sovereign grace. Their apostleship, their fruitfulness, their very friendship with Christ — none of it sprang from their choosing him, but all from his choosing them. And the choice was unto fruit: he appointed them to go, to bear lasting fruit, and to find their prayers answered. So the grace that chose them is the grace that makes them fruitful, and the priority of Christ’s love is the ground of all they are and do.
17:
“These things I command you, that ye love one another.” The witnesses note the recurrence of the central command, now repeated as the sum of all that has gone before. The grammatical-historical commentators take “these things” of the whole foregoing discourse — the vine, the abiding, the love, the choosing — all spoken “with this view, to promote brotherly love among them” (Gill). The command to love one another is thus not one theme among many but the goal toward which the rest converge; having taught the disciples to abide in him and to know themselves chosen and befriended, Christ binds the whole to mutual love. Ellicott and the others note how the thought of his love for them, which “exalted them from the position of slaves to friends,” is made to remind them again “of the duty of love to each other.”

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, makes the point most strongly: the fruit Christ appointed them to bear (v.16) is love. “Love is this fruit”; as Paul says, “the fruit of the Spirit is love” (Galatians 5:22), all other graces “springing from this source.” So Christ “commends love as if it were the only thing commanded; seeing that without it nothing can profit, with it nothing be wanting.” Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find here the practical heart of the chapter. The witnesses thus read the verse as the knot that ties the vine-discourse together. The abiding in Christ, the bearing of fruit, the dignity of friendship, the grace of being chosen — all issue in, and are proved by, love for one another. Mutual love is the fruit of the vine, the evidence of abiding, and the one command in which all the rest are summed. The disciple who has received so much love from Christ is bound, by all he has received, to love his brethren.

The witnesses observe that the threefold repetition of this command through the discourse (13:34; 15:12; 15:17) marks its weight. Christ will not let his disciples forget it; he returns to it again and again as the one thing he most desires of them. And he places it here, just before the warning of the world’s hatred, as the strength they will need: a company knit together in love can endure the enmity of the world that a divided company could not. Brotherly love is both the fruit of abiding and the armour against persecution.
18:
“If the world hate you, ye know that it hated me before it hated you.” The witnesses mark the turn from the inward love of the brethren to the outward hatred of the world. The grammatical-historical commentators note the deliberate contrast: having commanded mutual love, Christ now warns that the world will meet them with hatred, and prepares them for it (Gill, Henry). The comfort he gives is that they share his lot: “it hated me before it hated you,” so that their persecution is no strange thing but the world’s old enmity, first directed at the Master and now at the servants (Barnes, Ellicott). Robertson notes the form “if the world hate you” assumes the hatred as a fact to be reckoned with; the disciples must not be surprised by it.

Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find in the verse a needed forewarning. The disciples, having heard of love and joy and friendship, might forget that the world would oppose them; Christ steels them against the shock by setting their sufferings in the light of his own. The witnesses thus read the verse as the opening of the discourse on the world’s hatred. Christ does not hide from his own the cost of following him: the same world that hated and would crucify him will hate them too. But the warning is also a comfort. To be hated by the world is to be in the company of Christ, to bear the marks of belonging to him; the servant is not above his lord (v.20), and the hatred that falls on the disciple fell first on the Master. So the believer is prepared to meet the world’s enmity not with surprise or despair, but with the knowledge that it is the badge of his union with the hated and crucified Lord, who was hated before him.

The witnesses draw the comfort that the world’s hatred, far from disproving the disciples’ standing, confirms it. Were they of the world, the world would love its own; that it hates them shows they are Christ’s. So the verse turns a cause of fear into a ground of assurance, teaching the persecuted to read the enmity of the world not as a sign of God’s displeasure but as the mark of their fellowship with the Lord whom the world first hated. To suffer with him is to be known as his.
19:
“If ye were of the world, the world would love his own: but because ye are not of the world, but I have chosen you out of the world, therefore the world hateth you.” The witnesses explain the world’s hatred by the disciples’ separation from it. The grammatical-historical commentators note that “if ye were of the world” — actuated by its principles, “vain, earthly, sensual, given to pleasure, wealth, ambition” — “they would not oppose you” (Barnes). Ellicott catches the selfishness of the world’s love: it “would love not them, but that in them which was its own.” Robertson reads the second-class condition (the world does not love them, because they are not its own) and the “definite and specific reason for the world’s hatred of real Christians, whose very existence is a reproach to the sinful world” (cf. John 7:7; 1 John 3:13). Wesley sums it: “because your maxims, tempers, actions are quite opposite to theirs.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, turns the hatred into a consolation: “the reason why they are hated is, that they have been separated from the world; now this is their true happiness and glory, for in this manner they have been rescued from destruction.” If once they were of the world, “it is only by the mercy of God that they are distinguished from the rest who perish.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both the cause of the world’s hatred and its comfort. The cause is the disciples’ otherness: chosen out of the world, they no longer share its spirit, and their very holiness rebukes it, so that the world hates them as it hates the light that exposes its deeds. The comfort is that this hatred is the badge of their election — the proof that they have been taken out of the world by grace. Were they still of the world, it would love them as its own; that it hates them shows they belong to Christ. So the believer is taught to read the enmity of the world not as a misfortune but as a mark of the saving distinction grace has made.
20:
“Remember the word that I said unto you, The servant is not greater than his lord. If they have persecuted me, they will also persecute you; if they have kept my saying, they will keep yours also.” The witnesses take this as Christ steeling the disciples for persecution by his own example. The grammatical-historical commentators note the recalled saying (John 13:16; Matthew 10:24): since “the servant is not greater than his lord,” the disciples must expect the treatment their Master received. Robertson reads the imperative “remember” and the first-class condition “if they persecuted me” — and “they certainly did persecute” him (John 5:16) — so “they will persecute those like Jesus.” The verse also holds a quieter comfort: “if they have kept my saying, they will keep yours also” — some will receive the disciples’ word as some received Christ’s.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, makes Christ the supreme pattern of patient endurance: “the Lord, in exhorting His servants to endure with patience the hatred of the world, proposes to them no greater and better example than His own,” as Peter says, “Christ suffered for us, leaving us an example, that we should follow His steps” (1 Peter 2:21) — and this we do “by His assistance, who said, ‘Without me ye can do nothing.’” Calvin, for the Reformed, reads it as a confirmation of v.18: it would be “unreasonable that the condition of the servants should be better than that of the Master.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both a warning and a dignity. The warning is plain: the disciples will be persecuted as Christ was. The dignity is in the likeness: to suffer as the Master suffered is to be conformed to him, the servant sharing the lot of his Lord. And the verse balances the dark with the hopeful — as Christ’s word was both rejected and received, so the disciples’ preaching would meet both persecution and faith; some would hate, but some would keep their saying as they kept their Master’s.

The witnesses note the bracing honesty of the verse. Christ does not lure his disciples with promises of ease, but tells them plainly that the road they tread is the road he trod, and that the world which crucified him will not spare them. Yet in the very warning is comfort: their sufferings are not a sign of failure but of faithfulness, the proof that they belong to the persecuted Lord. To be treated as he was treated is the servant’s strange honour.
21:
“But all these things will they do unto you for my name’s sake, because they know not him that sent me.” The witnesses identify the cause of the persecution and the ignorance behind it. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that the disciples would suffer not as “thieves, murderers, and evildoers,” but “as Christians… for my name’s sake” (Gill, Robertson; cf. Matthew 10:22), the hatred falling on them as the representatives of their Lord. Ellicott notes that “the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistles are a commentary on this text.” The root of it is ignorance of God: Barnes explains that they “will not believe that God has sent me,” and Clarke makes this “the foundation of all religious persecution” — “those who are guilty of it… know nothing about God.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the strange madness of the world’s hatred: it is “so enraged against the doctrine of its own salvation” because it is “hurried on by blind ignorance to its own destruction; for no man would deliberately engage in battle against God.” Wesley draws the universal lesson: “in all ages and nations they who know not God will, for this cause, hate and persecute those that do.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both an ennobling of the disciples’ sufferings and an exposure of their persecutors. The sufferings are ennobled by their cause: to be hated “for my name’s sake” is to bear reproach for Christ, to suffer not as criminals but as his own. The persecutors are exposed in their ignorance: their hatred of the gospel, and of those who preach it, springs from not knowing the God who sent his Son. So the believer who suffers for Christ’s name is to count it honour, knowing that the enmity directed at him is the world’s blindness toward the Father, and falls on him only because he bears the name of the One the world will not know.

The witnesses draw the consolation that suffering ‘for his name’s sake’ transforms the meaning of persecution. What the world means as reproach becomes, in Christ’s reckoning, a badge of honour; the disciple bears the name of his Lord, and the blows aimed at that name fall on him as a mark of fellowship. So the verse arms the believer to meet the world’s enmity without surprise or shame, knowing that it springs from ignorance of God and falls on him only because he is Christ’s.
22:
“If I had not come and spoken unto them, they had not had sin: but now they have no cloke for their sin.” The witnesses are careful that this does not mean the Jews were sinless before Christ came, but speaks of one peculiar, aggravated sin — the rejection of the Messiah in the face of his revelation. The grammatical-historical commentators define it so: Lightfoot says the passage is “to be understood of that peculiar sin of rejecting the Messiah” — “if I had not spoken to them… they had not been guilty of this sin.” Barnes notes that Jesus had shown “by his own arguments” no less than by his miracles that he came from God, so that “by both these kinds of proof their guilt was to be measured.” Clarke renders “no cloke” as “no excuse” (cf. John 9:41): Christ had done such works as “demonstrated him to be the Messiah, yet they rejected him; here lay their sin.” Ellicott adds that, apart from this revelation, their sin “would have belonged to the times of ignorance, which God overlooked” (Acts 17:30).

The fathers and Reformers dwell on the aggravation that revelation brings. Chrysostom reads the verse as showing “the injustice of these persecutions”; Augustine names the sin “the sin of not believing Him, notwithstanding His doctrine and His miracles.” Calvin, for the Reformed, guards against making ignorance an excuse: their not knowing God (v.21) does not lighten their guilt, for “it is through malice that they are blind, just as if one were to shut his eyes that he might not be compelled to see the light.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a solemn truth about the responsibility that light brings. The coming and the word of Christ left the Jews without excuse; having seen and heard the Son of God, their rejection of him became a sin of a wholly different order from the sins of ignorance. The greater the revelation, the greater the guilt of refusing it; and those who had Christ himself among them, speaking and working as none other, could plead no cloke for spurning him.
23:
“He that hateth me hateth my Father also.” The witnesses take this brief, weighty saying as the deepest exposure of the world’s sin. The grammatical-historical commentators ground it in the union of the Father and the Son: since Christ reveals God (John 14:9), and “to dishonour Christ is to dishonour God” (Robertson; cf. John 5:23), the hatred of the one is the hatred of the other. Barnes presses the point against any thought that this was a slight crime: “a rejection of himself is also a rejection of God,” for “such is the union between them, that no one can hate the one without also hating the other.” Ellicott traces “the successive degrees of sin”: hatred of the disciples is hatred of the Master they represent; hatred of the Son is hatred of the Father he represents — “Hatred of the Father! There can be no greater darkness.”

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, raises the puzzle: how can they hate the Father, whom (v.21) they do not know? — and answers that they hate the true God under their false notion of him, refusing the God revealed in Christ. Gill adds that all the world’s hatred of Christ, his gospel, and his people “originally arises from that enmity that is naturally in the heart” against God. The witnesses thus find in the verse the unmasking of all hatred of Christ as hatred of God himself. Men may flatter themselves that they honour God while rejecting his Son, but Christ declares the two inseparable: to hate him is to hate the Father whose image and revealer he is. So the verse cuts off every pretence of piety that opposes Christ, and shows the world’s enmity for what it truly is — not a quarrel with a mere man, nor even with his disciples only, but enmity against the living God, whom none can hate in the Son and love in himself.

The witnesses press the verse as a test of all religion that claims to honour God while rejecting Christ. There is, says the Lord, no such honouring: to hate the Son is to hate the Father, for the two are one, and the Son is the Father’s revealer. So every form of piety, however sincere it imagines itself, that sets itself against Christ is shown to be, at bottom, enmity against God. The verse leaves no neutral ground between loving Christ and hating God.
24:
“If I had not done among them the works which none other man did, they had not had sin: but now have they both seen and hated both me and my Father.” The witnesses read this as the parallel to v.22 — the works, like the words, leaving the Jews without excuse. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Christ adds his miracles to his teaching as the double proof of his mission: he had done “the works which none other man did,” so that their rejection in the face of such evidence was the inexcusable sin (Gill, Barnes). The “none other man did” the commentators take of the unique number and character of his works — Augustine notes that, though prophets had raised the dead, “none… healed so many bodily” ailments as Christ. The terrible conclusion is that “they have both seen and hated both me and my Father” — having witnessed the works that revealed the Father in the Son, they hated what they saw.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, dwells on the strangeness of hating what one has seen to be good and divine; Calvin, for the Reformed, presses that this seeing aggravates the guilt, for they hated not in ignorance but against the clearest light. The witnesses thus find in the verse the second count of the world’s inexcusable sin. As Christ’s words took away all cloke (v.22), so his works take away the last excuse: men who had seen the very works of God, done as none other had done them, and yet hated the doer, hated with open eyes both the Son and the Father whom the works revealed. The witnesses note the gravity this lays upon all who reject a Christ they have seen and heard: where revelation is full, the guilt of unbelief is full; and to hate the Christ whose works are the works of God is to hate God himself, seen and refused.

The witnesses observe how the verse seals the case against unbelief. Word and work together — the teaching that none could match and the miracles that none could do — left the world without the least excuse. To have seen the works of God in Christ and still to hate him is to sin with open eyes against the fullest light. The verse thus measures guilt by light received, and warns that the clearest revelation, refused, brings the heaviest condemnation.
25:
“But this cometh to pass, that the word might be fulfilled that is written in their law, They hated me without a cause.” The witnesses read the world’s causeless hatred as the fulfilment of Scripture. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the citation: Robertson and Ellicott trace it to the Messianic Psalm (Psalm 69:4; also Psalm 35:19), noting that “law” here stands “for the whole of Scripture” (cf. John 10:34; John 12:34). Clarke observes that Psalm 69 “seems entirely prophetic of Christ” — his abasement, his crucifixion, the vengeance on Judea (cf. John 2:17; John 19:28; Acts 1:20). The key word is “without a cause” (dōrean): Barnes presses that “Jesus had broken no law, he had done no injury to his country or to any individual,” yet was hated — and “it is still true that sinners hate him in the same way,” though “he injures no one.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, marvels at the unreason of it: “nothing is more contrary to reason than to hate God,” yet such was “the malice with which their minds were envenomed.” He notes that the same had befallen David, whose words Christ quotes, but here the hatred reproves “the obstinate malice of the nation, which reigned perpetually from age to age… from grandfathers to grandchildren in unbroken succession.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both an explanation and a comfort. The explanation is that the world’s hatred, far from being outside God’s control, fulfils his word: the rejection of Christ was foretold in the Psalms, “part of the mysterious purpose of God.” The comfort is in the “without a cause”: the enmity Christ met, and that his people meet, is wholly undeserved — not provoked by any wrong in him, but the pure malice of a world that hates the light. So the believer who suffers causeless hatred is in the company of his Lord, whose causeless suffering the Scriptures themselves foretold.

The witnesses note how the verse turns even the world’s malice to the confirming of faith. That Christ was hated without a cause, foretold in the Psalms, shows that his rejection was no accident or defeat but the working-out of God’s declared purpose. So the believer who reads the Scriptures sees the very hatred that opposed his Lord written there beforehand, and finds in its fulfilment one more proof that Jesus is the Christ of whom the prophets spoke.
26:
“But when the Comforter is come, whom I will send unto you from the Father, even the Spirit of truth, which proceedeth from the Father, he shall testify of me.” The witnesses take this as the answer to the disciples’ fear: how shall they prevail against a world that hated and rejected even Christ? The grammatical-historical commentators note the great provision — the witness of the Spirit. Henry distinguishes “the principal testimony of the Spirit” here from “the subordinate testimony of the apostles” (v.27). The Eastern Chrysostom frames the disciples’ doubt — “if they have not been convinced… why do you send us to preach? How shall we be believed?” — and Christ’s answer: the Spirit “shall testify of Me.” On the procession, Robertson and Ellicott note the threefold testimony: the Father sends at the Son’s request (John 14:16), in the Son’s name (John 14:26), and here “I will send unto you from the Father” — “the Procession of the Holy Spirit from the Father and from the Son.”

Wesley, for the Wesleyan, draws the doctrine carefully: the Spirit’s “coming, and being sent by our Lord from the Father, to testify of him, are personal characters,” distinguishing him from Father and Son; and his title “the Spirit of truth,” with his “proceeding from the Father,” “can agree to none but a Divine person” — and that he proceeds from the Son as well “may be fairly argued.” Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the Spirit’s testimony the disciples’ stay against the world: “if their consciences rest on this testimony, they will never be shaken,” for no attack of the world “will be so violent as to shake” a faith grounded on the Spirit’s witness. The witnesses thus find in the verse the church’s great resource for its mission. Against a hostile world the disciples are not left to their own persuasion; the Spirit of truth, sent by the Son from the Father, proceeding from the Father, bears his own divine witness to Christ — in the hearts of the preachers and through them to the world. The testimony that converts is finally the Spirit’s, and it cannot fail.
27:
“And ye also shall bear witness, because ye have been with me from the beginning.” The witnesses take this as the subordinate, human testimony joined to the Spirit’s — the witness of the apostles as eyewitnesses. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the qualification: “because ye have been with me from the beginning” — from “his entrance on the public work of the ministry” (Barnes; cf. Matthew 4:17; Acts 1:21–22). For more than three years they had seen his works and heard his doctrine, and so were “qualified to bear witness of his character and doctrines.” Gill describes the content of their testimony — all they had seen with their eyes, heard with their ears, and handled of the word of life (cf. 1 John 1:1) — which “they could declare, and did declare.” Ellicott reads the present tense, “ye also bear witness,” and notes that the apostles themselves “distinguished between their own witness of things which had come within their own experience and the witness borne by the power of the Holy Spirit.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find in the two witnesses — the Spirit and the apostles — the double foundation of the church’s testimony to Christ. The witnesses thus read the verse as the completion of v.26. The Spirit testifies inwardly and with divine power; the apostles testify outwardly, as those who had companied with Christ from the first and could vouch for what they had seen. The two are not rivals but partners: the Spirit makes the apostles’ witness effectual, and the apostles’ witness is the appointed vehicle of the Spirit’s. So the gospel goes out into the hostile world borne by a twofold testimony — the eyewitness record of those who knew Christ in the flesh, carried home to the conscience by the Spirit of truth. The believer’s faith rests, then, not on human persuasion only, nor on private impression only, but on the apostolic witness sealed by the Spirit, against which the world’s hatred cannot finally prevail.

The witnesses underline that this twofold witness is the abiding pattern of the church’s testimony. The apostolic eyewitness, recorded in the Scriptures, supplies the outward and historical ground; the Spirit supplies the inward and convincing power. Neither stands alone: a record without the Spirit leaves the heart unmoved, and an impression without the record has no anchor. Together they bear an unanswerable testimony to Christ, which carries the gospel through every age and against every opposition of the world.
